item.title: The Blessed One saw the Venerable Khankhārevata sitting not far away, cross-legged, holding his body straight, contemplating his own purity through the overcoming of doubt.…
item.description:
The Blessed One saw the Venerable Khankhārevata sitting not far away, cross-legged, holding his body straight, contemplating his own purity through the overcoming of doubt. Then the Blessed One, having understood the meaning of this, on that occasion uttered this inspired utterance:

Whatever doubts there are
In this life or the next,
Not experienced by oneself
Or experienced by others,
A meditator abandons them all,
Completely.
Ardent, he lives the holy life.

From: Udāna 5.7
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Then, Bhāradvāja, I went to Āḷāra Kālāma and said: 'Friend Kālāma, is it to this extent that you have known for yourself, realized for yourself,…
item.description:
Then, Bhāradvāja, I went to Āḷāra Kālāma and said: 'Friend Kālāma, is it to this extent that you have known for yourself, realized for yourself, attained for yourself, and declared this Dhamma?' — 'Yes, friend, to this extent I have known, realized, attained, and declared this Dhamma.' — 'Friend, I too have known, realized, attained, and declare this Dhamma to this extent.' — 'It is a gain for us, friend, a great gain for us, that we see such a fellow celibate as the venerable one. The Dhamma that I have known, realized, attained, and declared — you too have known, realized, attained, and abide; the Dhamma that you have known, realized, attained, and abide — I too have known, realized, attained, and declared. The Dhamma I know, you know; the Dhamma you know, I know. As I am, so are you; as you are, so am I. Come now, friend, let the two of us take care of this assembly!' Thus, Bhāradvāja, Āḷāra Kālāma, who was my teacher, placed me, his disciple, on the same level as himself and honored me with supreme honor.

From: Majjhima Nikāya 100 – Saṅgāravasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Bhikkhus, there are these five benefits of walking meditation. What five? It endures long journeys; it endures exertion; it has few illnesses; it leads to…
item.description:
Bhikkhus, there are these five benefits of walking meditation. What five? It endures long journeys; it endures exertion; it has few illnesses; it leads to good digestion of food, drink, chewed, and tasted; the concentration attained while walking meditation lasts long. These, bhikkhus, are the five benefits of walking meditation.

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 5.29
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: The foolish, ignorant person Treats himself as an enemy, Doing evil deeds That bear bitter fruit. That deed is not well done Which, having been…
item.description:
The foolish, ignorant person
Treats himself as an enemy,
Doing evil deeds
That bear bitter fruit.

That deed is not well done
Which, having been done, one regrets,
And for which, with tearful face,
One weeps, receiving its result.

But that deed is well done
Which, having been done, one does not regret,
And for which, joyful and happy,
One receives its result.

The fool thinks it sweet
As long as evil has not ripened,
But when evil ripens,
The fool experiences suffering.

The evil deed done does not curdle immediately,
Like milk just milked;
Burning, it follows the fool,
Like fire covered with ash.

From: Dhammapada 60-75
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Not by fish or flesh, not by fasting, nakedness, shaven head, matted hair, ashes, rough deerskin, Not by tending the sacrificial fire, nor by many…
item.description:
Not by fish or flesh, not by fasting, nakedness, shaven head, matted hair, ashes, rough deerskin,
Not by tending the sacrificial fire, nor by many worldly austerities,
Nor by incantations, nor by seasonal sacrifices, does one become pure, if doubt is not overcome.

But by living with sense faculties guarded, restrained, established in Dhamma,
Loving uprightness and gentleness, beyond attachment, abandoning all suffering,
The wise one is not stained by things seen or heard.

From: Sutta Nipāta 2.2
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: When obsessed by hatred, brahmin, overcome, with mind possessed, one intends harm for oneself, intends harm for others, intends harm for both, and experiences mental…
item.description:
When obsessed by hatred, brahmin, overcome, with mind possessed, one intends harm for oneself, intends harm for others, intends harm for both, and experiences mental suffering and grief. When hatred is abandoned, one does not intend harm for oneself, does not intend harm for others, does not intend harm for both, and does not experience mental suffering and grief. When obsessed by hatred, overcome, with mind possessed, one does evil bodily deeds, evil verbal deeds, evil mental deeds. When hatred is abandoned, one does not do evil bodily deeds, evil verbal deeds, evil mental deeds. When obsessed by hatred, overcome, with mind possessed, one does not truly understand one's own good, nor the good of others, nor the good of both. When hatred is abandoned, one truly understands one's own good, the good of others, and the good of both. Thus, brahmin, the Dhamma is visible here and now, timeless, inviting inspection, leading onward, to be experienced by the wise for themselves.

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 3.54
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Upāli, for ten reasons are the training rules laid down for the disciples of the Tathāgata and the Pātimokkha is recited. What ten? For the…
item.description:
Upāli, for ten reasons are the training rules laid down for the disciples of the Tathāgata and the Pātimokkha is recited. What ten? For the excellence of the community, for the comfort of the community, for the restraint of the stubborn, for the comfortable dwelling of well-behaved bhikkhus, for the restraint of taints in this very life, for the prevention of taints in future lives, to inspire faith in those without faith, to increase faith in those with faith, for the duration of the good Dhamma, and for the support of the Discipline. For these ten reasons, Upāli, are the training rules laid down for the disciples of the Tathāgata and the Pātimokkha recited.

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 10.31
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Speaking of a heap of the unwholesome, bhikkhus, if one would speak rightly, one would speak of the five hindrances. For truly, bhikkhus, the entire…
item.description:
Speaking of a heap of the unwholesome, bhikkhus, if one would speak rightly, one would speak of the five hindrances. For truly, bhikkhus, the entire heap of the unwholesome is just the five hindrances. What five? The hindrance of sensual desire, the hindrance of ill-will, the hindrance of dullness and drowsiness, the hindrance of restlessness and remorse, the hindrance of doubt. Speaking of a heap of the unwholesome, bhikkhus, if one would speak rightly, one would speak of the five hindrances. For truly, bhikkhus, this entire heap of the unwholesome is just the five hindrances.

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 5.52
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Elder Usabha: Trees grow tall when irrigated by high rain clouds, With new rain they become green and strong. For Usabha, delighting in seclusion, aware…
item.description:
Elder Usabha:

Trees grow tall when irrigated by high rain clouds,
With new rain they become green and strong.
For Usabha, delighting in seclusion, aware of the forest,
That one produces many good things.

From: Theragāthā 1.110
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Kandaraka, in this Saṅgha of bhikkhus, there are bhikkhus who are learners, steadfast in virtue, steadfast in good conduct, intelligent, and who live with their…
item.description:
Kandaraka, in this Saṅgha of bhikkhus, there are bhikkhus who are learners, steadfast in virtue, steadfast in good conduct, intelligent, and who live with their minds well established in the Four Foundations of Mindfulness. What are the four? Here, Kandaraka, a bhikkhu dwells contemplating the body as a body, ardent, clearly comprehending, mindful, having removed covetousness and displeasure in regard to the world; he dwells contemplating feelings as feelings ... mind as mind ... phenomena as phenomena, ardent, clearly comprehending, mindful, having removed covetousness and displeasure in regard to the world.

From: Majjhima Nikāya 51 – Kandarakasutta
📖 Read this sutta
